questions.promptTex: \text{A shopkeeper bought a chair for } \$500 \text{ (its Cost Price)} \\ \text{and sold it for } \$650 \text{ (the Selling Price).} \\ \text{What is the profit percentage?}
hints: **Cost Price (CP)** is what the shopkeeper paid. **Selling Price (SP)** is what the customer paid.
**Profit = SP − CP** (the rupee/dollar amount of profit).
**Profit % = (Profit / CP) × 100** — always compute the percentage **on the CP**, not the SP.

steps.text: **Identify the values.** Cost Price (what the shopkeeper paid) = \$500. Selling Price (what the customer paid) = \$650.
**Convert to a percentage.** Profit % is always measured *as a fraction of the Cost Price*:

questions.promptTex: \text{A trader buys an item at Cost Price (CP) } \$800. \\ \text{She wants a } 15\% \text{ profit.} \\ \text{At what Selling Price (SP) should she sell it (in dollars)?}
hints: **Selling Price (SP)** = Cost Price + Profit, where profit is 15% of CP.
Formula: $SP = CP \times \left(1 + \dfrac{\text{Profit \%}}{100}\right)$.

questions.promptTex: \text{An item is sold at Selling Price (SP) } \$576 \text{, making a } 20\% \text{ profit.} \\ \text{What was its Cost Price (CP) in dollars?}
hints: **Common mistake:** to find CP, students often subtract 20% from SP. That's WRONG — the 20% is computed *on the CP*, not on the SP.
Use the formula in reverse: $CP = \dfrac{SP}{1 + P\%/100} = \dfrac{576}{1.20}$.

steps.text: **Start from the SP formula** and solve for CP:

questions.promptTex: \text{An item has Marked Price (MP) } \$1500 \text{ on its tag.} \\ \text{The store offers a } 20\% \text{ discount.} \\ \text{What is the Selling Price (SP) in dollars?}
hints: **Marked Price (MP)** is the price on the tag *before* any discount.
**Discount %** is computed on the MP (NOT the SP).
Formula: $SP = MP \times \left(1 - \dfrac{D\%}{100}\right)$.

questions.promptTex: \text{A store gives two successive discounts: first } 10\%, \text{ then } 20\%. \\ \text{What single discount percentage gives the same final price?}
hints: **Watch out:** $10\% + 20\% \neq 30\%$. Successive discounts are multiplied, not added.
If MP is \$100: after 10% off → \$90; after another 20% off \$90 → \$72. Total saved \$28 → 28% discount overall.
**Shortcut formula** for two successive discounts $a\%$ and $b\%$: equivalent single = $a + b - \dfrac{ab}{100}$.

steps.text: **Verify with concrete numbers.** Take MP = \$100. After 10% off: \$90. After 20% off \$90: $90 \times 0.80 = \$72$. Discount of \$28 on \$100 = 28%. ✓

questions.promptTex: \text{A shopkeeper marks an item at } 40\% \text{ above its Cost Price (CP),} \\ \text{and then offers a } 25\% \text{ discount on this Marked Price.} \\ \text{What is the shopkeeper's actual profit percentage?}
hints: Pick a friendly CP — say \$100 — since we're after a percentage.
**Step 1:** Markup 40% means MP = $100 \times 1.40 = \$140$.
**Step 2:** Discount 25% on MP means SP = $140 \times 0.75 = \$105$.
**Step 3:** Profit = $105 - 100 = \$5$, so profit % = 5%.
questions.explanation: Take $CP = \$100$. After 40% markup, $MP = \$140$. After 25% discount, $SP = 140 \times 0.75 = \$105$. Profit = \$5, so profit % = 5%.

steps.text: **Pick CP = \$100** (since the answer is a percentage, the actual CP value doesn't matter).
**Apply the 40% markup** to get the Marked Price:
**Apply the 25% discount** to get the Selling Price:

questions.promptTex: \text{A trader buys an item at Cost Price (CP) } \$240. \\ \text{He plans to offer a } 20\% \text{ discount but still wants } 25\% \text{ profit.} \\ \text{What Marked Price (MP) should he set, in dollars?}
hints: **Work backwards.** First find the target Selling Price (SP) that yields 25% profit: $SP = 240 \times 1.25 = \$300$.
**Then back-compute MP** from the discount: $MP \times 0.80 = 300$, so $MP = 300 / 0.80 = \$375$.
questions.explanation: Target SP = $240 \times 1.25 = \$300$. To get this after 20% discount: $MP \times 0.80 = 300 \Rightarrow MP = \$375$.

steps.text: **Step 1 — Find target SP** (for 25% profit on CP = \$240):
**Step 2 — MP must give this SP after a 20% discount.** So $SP = MP \times 0.80$:

questions.promptTex: \text{A trader sells two articles at } \$1200 \text{ each (the same Selling Price for both).} \\ \text{On one she makes } 20\% \text{ gain, on the other } 20\% \text{ loss.} \\ \text{Find her net loss in dollars (absolute value).}
hints: **Don't fall for the symmetry illusion.** Equal-and-opposite percentages do NOT cancel because they're applied to *different* Cost Prices.
**Compare totals.** Total spent: $\$1000 + \$1500 = \$2500$. Total received: $\$1200 + \$1200 = \$2400$. Loss = \$100.

steps.text: **Why the loss?** Equal percentages on different bases don't cancel — the 20% loss is on a larger CP than the 20% gain.

questions.promptTex: \text{A shirt's Marked Price (MP) is } \$800. \\ \text{It is sold for } \$680. \\ \text{What is the discount percentage?}
hints: **Discount** = MP − SP (in dollars). **Discount %** = (Discount / MP) × 100.

questions.promptTex: \text{A car costs (CP) } \$400{,}000 \text{ and is sold at a } 15\% \text{ loss.} \\ \text{Find the Selling Price (SP) in dollars.}
hints: **Loss** means SP is below CP. Formula: $SP = CP \times \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{Loss \%}}{100}\right)$.

steps.text: **For a loss**, multiply CP by $(1 - L\%/100)$:

questions.promptTex: \text{An item sold at a particular Selling Price gives a profit of } \$300. \\ \text{If the SP is doubled, the profit becomes } \$900. \\ \text{Find the Cost Price (CP) in dollars.}
hints: **Let CP = $x$.** Original SP = $x + 300$. Doubled SP = $2(x + 300) = 2x + 600$.
**Doubled SP gives profit \$900** means $(2x + 600) - x = 900$.
$x + 600 = 900 \Rightarrow x = \$300$.
questions.explanation: Let CP = $x$. Original SP = $x + 300$. Doubled SP = $2x + 600$ and profit = $(2x + 600) - x = x + 600 = 900$. So $x = \$300$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Three successive discounts of } 20\%, 10\%, \text{ and } 5\% \text{ are applied to an item.} \\ \text{What single discount percentage is equivalent? (Round to 1 decimal.)}
hints: **Successive discounts multiply (don't add).** Each discount applies to the price *after* the previous one.
If MP = \$1, the remaining fraction after all three is $0.80 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.684$.
**Equivalent single discount** = $1 - 0.684 = 0.316 = 31.6\%$.

steps.text: **For successive discounts**, multiply the surviving fractions (1 minus each discount):

questions.promptTex: \text{If a profit of } 20\% \text{ is measured \emph{on the Selling Price}}, \\ \text{what is the profit percentage on the Cost Price (the standard convention)?}
hints: **Standard convention:** profit % is always taken on CP, not SP. This problem is asking us to convert.
**Pick SP = \$100.** Then profit = 20% of SP = \$20. So CP = $100 - 20 = \$80$.
**Profit % on CP** = $\dfrac{20}{80} \times 100 = 25\%$.
questions.explanation: Take SP = \$100. Profit on SP = \$20, so CP = \$80. Profit % on CP = $20/80 \times 100 = 25\%$.

steps.text: **Pick a convenient SP** = \$100 (since we're after a percentage).

questions.promptTex: \text{An item is first marked up by } 25\% \text{ above CP, then a } 20\% \text{ discount is applied.} \\ \text{What is the net change percentage relative to the original CP?}
hints: **Set CP = \$100** (since we want a percentage).
**After 20% discount on \$125:** $125 \times 0.80 = \$100$. **Same as CP!**
**Net change = 0%.** (Because $1.25 \times 0.80 = 1.00$ exactly.)

steps.text: **Back to where we started — net change = 0%.** The two factors $1.25$ and $0.80$ multiply to exactly $1$.

questions.promptTex: \text{A dishonest grocer claims to sell rice 'at Cost Price' but uses a } 900 \text{ g weight when} \\ \text{customers ask for 1 kg. What is his actual profit percentage?} \\ \text{(Round to 2 decimals.)}
hints: **The trick:** he charges the customer for 1000 g, but only delivers 900 g. His real CP is the cost of 900 g.
Suppose rice costs \$1 per gram. He pays \$900 (his real CP) and collects \$1000 (his SP).
**Profit % on his real CP** = $\dfrac{1000 - 900}{900} \times 100 = \dfrac{100}{9}\% \approx 11.11\%$.
questions.explanation: He pays for 900 g (real CP) but charges for 1000 g (SP). Profit = 100/900 × 100% ≈ 11.11%.

steps.text: **Spot the cheat.** The grocer's true Cost Price is the cost of the 900 g he actually hands over. The Selling Price is what he charges (price of 1000 g).
**Assume unit cost** \$1 per gram. Then his CP = \$900 and SP = \$1000.
